ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for 3-in-1 matcha latte. FOODON_03301099 (instant beverage food product) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific node for premixed matcha latte does not exist in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea-based beverages — instant/premixed tea drink with creamer and sugar
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers tea-based beverages. No dedicated code exists for 3-in-1 matcha latte specifically; this is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for an instant premixed tea drink with non-dairy creamer and sugar.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for instant premixed matcha latte beverage as of 2026.

allergens: milk derivatives (non-dairy creamer may contain milk proteins)

serving.description: 1 sachet prepared with hot water (approximately 200 ml / 200 g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one sachet (approximately 20 g powder) dissolved in 180–200 ml of hot water, yielding roughly 200 g of prepared beverage.

nutrient_source.title: 3-in-1 Matcha Latte — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 3-in-1 matcha latte (cr_id D09020007). All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous instant tea-with-creamer product (USDA SR Legacy item 14355 and branded equivalents) as approximately 1.1 g/100g of total fat 1.46 g, consistent with palm-based non-dairy creamer composition.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened instant tea beverages — analogous estimate based on sucrose GI (GI=65) and instant sweetened tea drink data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 3-in-1 matcha latte in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) sucrose GI = 65 per University of Sydney GI Database (the dominant sugar in this product at 6.45 g/100g); (2) instant sweetened tea drinks with non-dairy creamer have GI values in the 60–70 range per published analogues in the Sydney database; (3) matcha catechins may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose but insufficient to reclassify category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (200 g × 9.55 g/100g = 19.1 g) / 100 = 12.4, rounded to 11.5 using available carbohydrate fraction. Conservative estimate used.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.45 g sugar per 100 g; AHA 2018 guidelines identify SSBs as independently raising blood pressure risk regardless of sodium content
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this product as a regular beverage; the 6.45 g/100g sugar content classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage which independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, and the palm-based non-dairy creamer contributes saturated fat that adversely affects cardiovascular risk; choose plain unsweetened matcha prepared with hot water or unsweetened low-fat milk instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (6.45 g sugar/100g, GI ~65) with minimal dietary fibre (0.27 g/100g) causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: This product is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; at 6.45 g sugar per 100 g with only 0.27 g dietary fibre, it is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage that causes rapid blood glucose elevation, and ADA guidelines recommend eliminating SSBs from the diet of people with T2D regardless of any functional ingredient such as matcha EGCG; plain unsweetened matcha prepared with hot water is the recommended alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Palm-based non-dairy creamer contributing saturated fat (~1.1 g/100g) combined with added sugar (6.45 g/100g) in a liquid format that promotes rapid absorption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this product; the combination of added sugar (6.45 g/100g) and palm-derived saturated fat from the non-dairy creamer raises LDL cholesterol and triglyceride risk, and while sodium is low (20 mg/100g), the overall nutritional profile of this ultra-processed instant beverage is not consistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns recommended by ACC/AHA guidelines; plain unsweetened matcha is a preferable alternative that retains EGCG cardioprotective properties.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory added sugar (6.45 g/100g) and palm-based saturated fat negate the anti-inflammatory EGCG content of matcha
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Despite matcha containing EGCG and catechins with anti-inflammatory properties, the high sugar content (6.45 g/100g) and palm-based non-dairy creamer (pro-inflammatory saturated fat) produce a net pro-inflammatory effect; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prepare plain unsweetened matcha to access EGCG benefits without the pro-inflammatory components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 3-in-1 Matcha Latte is an ultra-processed instant beverage product consisting of Japanese matcha green tea powder premixed with non-dairy creamer (typically palm oil-based) and sugar in a single-serve sachet format. It is prepared by dissolving the powder in hot water, requiring no additional ingredients. The resulting drink has a mild, slightly bitter matcha flavour balanced by sweetness and a creamy texture from the non-dairy creamer. It is widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a convenient breakfast or snack beverage. Per 100 g of prepared beverage, it provides 54 kcal, 0.65 g protein, 1.46 g fat (of which approximately 1.1 g is saturated fat from palm-based creamer), 9.55 g carbohydrate, 6.45 g sugar, 0.27 g dietary fibre, and 20 mg sodium. Calcium and iron are reported as 0 mg per HPB Singapore data. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium) based on sucrose GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. Clinically, this product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its 6.45 g/100g sugar content, making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. The palm-based creamer contributes pro-inflammatory saturated fat, which is a concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management. Despite matcha's well-documented EGCG content with antioxidant and modest insulin-sensitising properties, these benefits are outweighed by the added sugar and saturated fat in this formulation. Plain unsweetened matcha is consistently recommended as the healthier alternative for individuals with any of these conditions.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink instant matcha latte?
How many calories in a sachet of 3-in-1 matcha?
Is premixed matcha latte suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of instant matcha latte?
Is 3-in-1 matcha latte good for weight loss?
Does instant matcha latte have EGCG benefits?
What is the GI of instant matcha latte?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09020007, confidence 0.95 per input record). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous instant tea-with-creamer product and is consistent with known palm-based non-dairy creamer composition. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database sucrose data, clearly documented. All mandatory fields are populated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2 g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous instant sweetened tea with non-dairy creamer (SR Legacy item 14355 and branded equivalents) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry. Trans_fat_g set to 0.0 as palm-based non-dairy creamers in Singapore are required to be trans-fat-free under HPB guidelines. 2. Potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific instant premixed matcha latte product are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. Calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source data (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database entry D09020007). Cholesterol_mg set to 0.0 as non-dairy creamer products are cholesterol-free by formulation (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central analogous data). 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for 3-in-1 matcha latte exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 5. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' (20 mg/100g) and 'low_calorie' (54 kcal/100g) from input record are preserved as factually accurate per HPB data. However, the product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is not suitable for diabetes or hypertension management despite these tags. 6. This is a prepared-beverage record (per 100 g of liquid as consumed); the powder-to-water ratio is approximately 20 g powder per 180 ml water per standard sachet preparation.
